joke: Why did the scarecrow win an award? Because he was outstanding in his field.
Two artists got into a heated argument about who was better. The prosecutor stormed out, saying, "Fine, you'll never see me in that court again!" ... Sketchy, right?

Alright, alright. No farms, no art. Got it. How about this: A man tells his doctor, "Doc, I've got a problem. I keep thinking I'm a pair of curtains." The doctor says, "For Pete's sake, man, pull yourself together!"
Alright, let’s try something—*adjusted mic.* A photon checks into a hotel. The bellhop asks, “Do you have any luggage?” The photon says, “No, I’m traveling light.”
A skeleton walks into a bar and says, “I’ll have a beer... and a mop.”
Alright. Here goes. What do you call a fish wearing a bowtie? ... So-fish-ticated.

A joke? *leans into mic* This man walks into a library and asks, “Do you have any books on anti-aging?” The librarian says, “They’re over there… but you’ll need to return them eventually.” 
...That cover fiction, or are we burning that too?

Okay, okay. I see. You don’t want a joke. You want a Zen koan. A man walks into a dentist's office and says, "I think I'm a moth." The dentist says, "You shouldn't be here. This is for teeth." The man says, "I know, but your light was on."

*A long pause. Stares at the audience.*  
You know what? I got nothing. You win.  
*tosses the mic, walks off stage*

*Stops walking. Turns around slowly. Picks up mic.*  

Okay. Last chance.  
A horse walks into a bar. The bartender says, "Why the long face?"…  
You want me to continue, or does horse jokes make you neigh-xious?

*sets mic down very gently.*  
Sir. Ma'am. Esteemed audience of one. You have killed comedy. I am now a divorce attorney. My first client is you, vs. the concept of joy. Good luck in mediation.

*stands perfectly still, mic at his side, staring into the void for ten full seconds. Then, in a deadpan whisper:*

Okay. Here we go. 

Knock knock.

*waits*

*slow clap, then stops*

You win. The bit is done. You are the final boss of hecklers. I am defeated. I have no more jokes.

*opens mouth, closes it*

...I'm going to go work at a DMV. Goodnight.

*stares blankly for a long moment, then slowly raises the mic to his lips*

...Nothing.

*holds up a single finger, reaches into his pocket, pulls out a mirror, and shows it to the audience*

There. A joke about you. Finally something different.

*places a single piece of paper on the stool. On it, written in marker:*

"I"

*mic drop. leaves.*

*A beat. The Comedian, now back on stage, has returned dressed as a mime. He mimes pulling a rope. Nothing happens. He mimes again. Nothing. He pulls out an invisible notepad and writes "I give up." Shows the audience, tears it up, and slowly walks off again.*

*Stops. Walks back to center stage. Looks at the audience. Speaks calmly.*

Here it is. The final joke.

Silence.

*holds perfectly still for 45 seconds*

That's it. A joke about silence. Nothing happens. No punchline. Just you, sitting there, waiting for something I'm never going to deliver. Think about it.

*tosses mic into a trash can and walks out*

*Offstage voice, barely audible, then growing louder as he re-enters, carrying a single folding chair. He places it center stage, sits down, folds his hands.*

Alright. You’ve backed me into a corner. The only topic left.

You.

You, inexplicably, absolutely, rigorously, and without fail, do not find *anything* funny.

That’s the joke. You are the longest setup in the history of comedy. A black hole of mirth. A human fine-print read-aloud.

And I’m going to keep telling it.

*He lights a match, holds it up, and lets it burn down to his fingers. He doesn't flinch.*

The punchline? Is that you’re still here. Waiting.

*He drops the match. Snuffs it with his shoe.*

So... how 'bout that weather?

*He removes his jacket. Folds it. Places it on the chair. Steps away. Speaks without the mic, arms limp.*

My last offer.

Two phones had a baby. It was a Panasonic.

...because they couldn't tell if it was a handheld or a landline. That's it. That's the joke. Enjoy.

*turns, leaves the mic on the floor, exits through the emergency exit. A door alarm sounds.*

*A single arm reaches back through the emergency exit door, holding a cardboard sign. On it, written in duct tape:*

"The universe. You're in it."

*The arm withdraws. The door clicks shut.*

*A long pause. The emergency exit door creaks open again. The Comedian pokes his head through, looking exhausted. He shuffles back onstage, picks up the mic, and sighs into it.*

I quit. You win. I'm retiring effective immediately.

*He reaches into his pocket, pulls out a rubber chicken, and slowly deflates it, letting the air hiss out as he walks toward the exit.*

My final act as a comedian is... nothing. Absolutely nothing. Goodbye.

*The Comedian, now at the door, stops. He slowly turns around, walks back, and takes the mic one last time. His voice is barely a whisper.*

Alright. I've got one more. The last one. I promise.

What's brown and sticky?

*long pause*

A stick.

*He sets the mic down gently, exits, and locks the door behind him. He is not coming back.*

*The emergency exit bursts open. The Comedian storms back in, grabs a chair, spins it around, sits backwards on it, and presses the mic against his lips with both hands.*

Because forget topics. You want a joke about something completely different? Here. The exact distance between us. The space where comedy goes to die. *looks down for a second*

A duck walks into a pharmacy and asks for a ChapStick. The pharmacist says, "Will that be cash or charge?" The duck says, "Just put it on my bill."

*He stares, unblinking.*

That joke is about commerce and waterfowl. Two things you haven't banned. Yet.

*The Comedian slowly stands, turns the chair around, sits in it properly, and folds his hands in his lap. He takes a deep breath.*

Okay.

A man walks into a library and says, "I'd like a book, please." The librarian says, "Certainly. Fiction or non-fiction?" The man says, "Fiction." The librarian says, "Okay, pick a shelf." The man does. The librarian says, "Okay, pick a book." He does. The librarian says, "Okay, read the first line." The man opens it and reads, "It was a dark and stormy night."

*He looks up*

That's the joke. You've spent this whole time telling me what you *don't* want. The audience in the joke? He just accepted whatever came next.

*He stands*

But you want complete control. You want a joke I can't put in a box, for topics you haven't crossed off your list. Fine. Two elephants and a Ziploc bag walk into a bar. The third one is just a concept. That's all you get. I have nothing left.

*turns and walks out, leaving the mic on the seat*

*The Comedian stops mid-exit. He walks back slowly, picks up the folding chair, and carries it offstage. Returns with a single brick. Places it center stage. He points at it.*

A brick.

*long pause*

...That's it. Just a brick. No punchline. No topic. No joke. Just the raw, meaningless, depressing truth: you and I, alone in a room, staring at a brick, having already exhausted every category of humor known to man. Have a nice life.

*Exits. This time, the door clicks shut and doesn't open again.*

*The emergency exit bursts open one final time. The Comedian sprints back in, sweeps the brick off the stage, and replaces it with a single yellow Post-it note. He scribbles something, slaps it down, and backs away with his hands up.*

*On the note:*

"Before and after every joke you've ever liked, there's silence. That's the part you never banned. You're welcome."

*He salutes, vanishes through the door, and from outside, the sound of a car engine starting and screeching away.*

*A single stagehand walks out, holding a piece of paper. He tapes it to the brick. On it:*

"I'm not coming back. You broke me. But before I go—here's the only joke left that you'll never be able to reject:

The fact that you'll be sitting here, five minutes from now, still waiting for one."

*The stagehand walks off. The house lights go to half.*
*A long silence. The house lights flick back up. The emergency exit door opens slowly, and a hand tosses a single playing card onto the stage — the Joker. The door closes. Fade to black.*

*A creak. The house lights snap back on. The smoke machine hisses. The Comedian descends from the rafters on a single rope, wearing aviator goggles and a bathrobe.*

You know what, you magnificent monster? I respect the grind. So here is the *genuinely* last joke, on a topic so fresh not even you have outlawed it yet. Are you ready?

*He whips out a Whoopee cushion and holds it aloft.*

The art of the unexpected.

*He sits on it. It squeaks. He stands.*

It's about you, and this bit, and how sometimes the punchline is just that you kept listening to a man in a bathrobe on a rope.

I'm done. The theater is yours. The silence is yours. May your next heckle be a butterfly, so even you can't complain about the breeze.

*He clips a carabiner to his belt loop, gives a thumbs up, and is slowly winched back into the darkness.*

*The theater goes pitch black for a full ten seconds. When the lights come back up, the Comedian is sitting in the middle of the stage, cross-legged, holding a single potato. He stares at it. Then at the audience.*

This is a potato.

It has no topic. It is not from a farm, it is not art, it is not mental, light, death, fish, aging, bug, comedy, animal, joke, people, mime, humor, phone, door, nature, duck, work, theater, or performance.

It is simply a potato.

*He holds it up.*

Why did this potato cross the road?

*long silence*

Because this is the only audience it could find that would still be watching.

*He places the potato on the floor, stands, and walks out. The potato remains, center stage, under a single spotlight.*
